text: Later on, the king of the Ammonites died, and his son Hanun became king in his place.
David thought, "I will treat Hanun kindly, just as his father Nahash was kind to me." So David sent envoys to comfort Hanun over his father's death, and they arrived in Ammonite territory.

But the Ammonite officials said to Hanun their lord, "Do you really think David is honoring your father by sending men to comfort you? Surely he has sent them to scout the city and spy it out before attacking it."
So Hanun seized David's envoys, shaved off half of each man's beard, cut their clothing off at the hips, and sent them away.
When David heard of it, he sent messengers to meet them, since the men were deeply humiliated. "Stay in Jericho," the king told them, "until your beards grow back, and then come home."

Realizing they had made themselves hated by David, the Ammonites hired Aramean troops from Beth-rehob and Zobah, twenty thousand foot soldiers, plus a thousand men from the king of Maacah and twelve thousand from the men of Tob.
When David learned of this, he sent Joab out with the whole army of fighting men.
The Ammonites came out and lined up for battle at the entrance of their city gate, while the Arameans from Zobah, Rehob, Tob, and Maacah took position separately out in the open country.
Seeing that he was facing enemies both ahead and behind, Joab chose his best Israelite troops and lined them up against the Arameans.
He put the rest of the army under his brother Abishai, who arranged them to face the Ammonites.
Joab said, "If the Arameans prove too strong for me, come and help me. But if the Ammonites are too strong for you, I will come and help you.
Be strong — let's fight bravely for our people and for the cities of our God. May the LORD do whatever seems right to him."
Then Joab and his troops advanced against the Arameans, and the Arameans fled before them.
When the Ammonites saw the Arameans running away, they too fled from Abishai and retreated into the city. So Joab pulled back from the Ammonites and returned to Jerusalem.
Once the Arameans realized Israel had beaten them, they regrouped their forces.
Hadadezer sent for the Aramean troops from across the Euphrates, and they came to Helam, led by Shobach, the commander of Hadadezer's army.
When David was told of this, he gathered all Israel, crossed the Jordan, and marched to Helam. The Arameans formed their battle lines to meet David and fought against him.
But the Arameans fled before Israel, and David struck down the crews of seven hundred chariots and forty thousand horsemen; he also wounded Shobach the army commander, and he died there.
When all the kings who served Hadadezer saw they had been defeated by Israel, they made peace with Israel and submitted to them. After that, the Arameans were afraid to help the Ammonites again.
